In the [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") period in Mesopotamia, [Halafian pottery](/wiki/Halaf_culture#Halaf_pottery "Halaf culture") achieved a level of technical competence and sophistication, not seen until the later developments of [Greek pottery](/wiki/Greek_pottery "Greek pottery") with Corinthian and [Attic ware](/wiki/Attic_ware "Attic ware").

#### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Europe")]

Main articles: [Minoan pottery](/wiki/Minoan_pottery "Minoan pottery"), [Pottery of ancient Greece](/wiki/Pottery_of_ancient_Greece "Pottery of ancient Greece"), and [Ancient Roman pottery](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_pottery "Ancient Roman pottery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Altamura_Painter_-_Red-Figure_Calyx_Krater_-_Walters_48262_-_Side_A.jpg/250px-Altamura_Painter_-_Red-Figure_Calyx_Krater_-_Walters_48262_-_Side_A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Altamura_Painter_-_Red-Figure_Calyx_Krater_-_Walters_48262_-_Side_A.jpg)

Greek [red-figure](/wiki/Red-figure_pottery "Red-figure pottery") vase in the [krater](/wiki/Krater "Krater") shape, between 470 and 460 BC, by the [Altamura Painter](/wiki/Altamura_Painter "Altamura Painter")

Europe's oldest pottery, dating from circa 6700 BC, was found on the banks of the [Samara River](/wiki/Samara_(Volga) "Samara (Volga)") in the middle [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga") region of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[125]](#cite_note-125) These sites are known as the [Yelshanka culture](/wiki/Yelshanka_culture "Yelshanka culture").

The early inhabitants of Europe developed pottery in the [Linear Pottery culture](/wiki/Linear_Pottery_culture "Linear Pottery culture") slightly later than the Near East, circa 5500–4500 BC. In the ancient Western Mediterranean, elaborately painted earthenware reached very high levels of artistic achievement in the Greek world; numerous survivals from tombs remain. Minoan pottery was characterized by complex painted decoration with natural themes.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Classical Greek culture began to emerge around 1000 BC, featuring a variety of well-crafted pottery that now included the human form as a decorative motif. The pottery wheel was now in regular use. Although glazing was known to these potters, it was not widely used. Instead, a more porous clay slip was used for decoration. A wide [range of shapes](/wiki/Typology_of_Greek_vase_shapes "Typology of Greek vase shapes") for different uses developed early and remained essentially unchanged during Greek history.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Fine [Etruscan pottery](/wiki/Etruscan_pottery "Etruscan pottery") was heavily influenced by Greek pottery and often imported Greek potters and painters. [Ancient Roman pottery](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_pottery "Ancient Roman pottery") made much less use of painting but relied on moulded decoration, enabling industrial-scale production. Much of the so-called red [Samian ware](/wiki/Samian_ware "Samian ware") of the Early [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") was produced in modern Germany and France, where entrepreneurs established large potteries. Excavations at [Augusta Raurica](/wiki/Augusta_Raurica "Augusta Raurica"), near Basel, Switzerland, have revealed a pottery production site in use from the 1st to the 4th century AD.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

Pottery was hardly seen on the tables of elites from [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic "Hellenistic") times until the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), and most medieval wares were coarse and utilitarian, as the elites ate off metal vessels. Painted [Hispano-Moresque ware](/wiki/Hispano-Moresque_ware "Hispano-Moresque ware") from Spain, developing the styles of [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"), became a luxury for late medieval elites, and was adapted in Italy into *[maiolica](/wiki/Maiolica "Maiolica")* in the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance"). Both of these were [faience](/wiki/Faience "Faience") or [tin-glazed](/wiki/Tin-glazed "Tin-glazed") earthenware, and fine faience continued to be made until around 1800 in various countries, especially France, with [Nevers faience](/wiki/Nevers_faience "Nevers faience") and several other centres. In the 17th century, imports of [Chinese export porcelain](/wiki/Chinese_export_porcelain "Chinese export porcelain") and its [Japanese equivalent](/wiki/Japanese_export_porcelain "Japanese export porcelain") raised the market
expectations of fine pottery, and European manufacturers eventually learned to make porcelain, often in the form of [soft-paste porcelain](/wiki/Soft-paste_porcelain "Soft-paste porcelain"). From the 18th century onward, European porcelain and other wares from many producers became extremely popular, reducing Asian imports.

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main articles: [Wedgwood](/wiki/Wedgwood "Wedgwood"), [Staffordshire figure](/wiki/Staffordshire_figure "Staffordshire figure"), [Royal Doulton](/wiki/Royal_Doulton "Royal Doulton"), [Mintons](/wiki/Mintons "Mintons"), [Midwinter Pottery](/wiki/Midwinter_Pottery "Midwinter Pottery"), and [Spode](/wiki/Spode "Spode")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/BLW_Bone_China_Chocolate_Cup.jpg/250px-BLW_Bone_China_Chocolate_Cup.jpg)](/wiki/File:BLW_Bone_China_Chocolate_Cup.jpg)

Handpainted bone china cup. England, 1815–1820

The city of [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") is widely known as "The Potteries" because of the large number of pottery factories or, colloquially, "Pot Banks". It was one of the first industrial cities of the modern era, where, as early as 1785, 200 pottery manufacturers employed 20,000 workers.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) [Josiah Wedgwood](/wiki/Josiah_Wedgwood "Josiah Wedgwood") (1730–1795) was the dominant leader.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

In North Staffordshire, hundreds of companies produced all kinds of pottery, from tablewares and decorative pieces to industrial items. The main pottery types of earthenware, stoneware, and porcelain were all made in large quantities. The Staffordshire industry was a major innovator in developing new varieties of ceramic bodies, such as [bone china](/wiki/Bone_china "Bone china") and [jasperware](/wiki/Jasperware "Jasperware"), and in pioneering [transfer printing](/wiki/Transfer_printing "Transfer printing") and other glazing and decorating techniques. In general, Staffordshire was strongest in the middle and lower price ranges, though it also produced the finest and most expensive wares.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

By the late 18th century, North Staffordshire was the largest producer of ceramics in the UK, despite significant hubs elsewhere. Large export markets took Staffordshire pottery around the world, especially in the 19th century.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Production had begun to decline in the late 19th century, as other countries developed their industries, and declined notably after World War II. Employment fell from 45,000 in 1975 to 23,000 in 1991 and to 13,000 in 2002.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

#### Arabic pottery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Arabic pottery")]

Main articles: [Islamic pottery](/wiki/Islamic_pottery "Islamic pottery") and [Persian pottery](/wiki/Persian_pottery "Persian pottery")

Early [Islamic pottery](/wiki/Islamic_pottery "Islamic pottery") followed the forms of the regions which the Arabs conquered. Eventually, however, there was cross-fertilization between the regions. This was most notable in the [Chinese influences on Islamic pottery](/wiki/Chinese_influences_on_Islamic_pottery "Chinese influences on Islamic pottery"). Trade between China and the Islamic world occurred through a network of trading posts along the lengthy [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"). Middle Eastern nations imported stoneware and later porcelain from China. China imported the minerals for [Cobalt blue](/wiki/Cobalt_blue "Cobalt blue") from the Islamic-ruled [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") to decorate their [blue and white porcelain](/wiki/Blue_and_white_porcelain "Blue and white porcelain"), which they then exported to the Islamic world.

Likewise, Arabic art contributed to a lasting pottery form identified as [Hispano-Moresque](/wiki/Hispano-Moresque "Hispano-Moresque") in [Andalucia](/wiki/Andalucia "Andalucia"). Unique Islamic forms were also developed, including [fritware](/wiki/Fritware "Fritware"), [lusterware](/wiki/Lusterware "Lusterware"), and specialized glazes such as [tin-glazing](/wiki/Tin-glazing "Tin-glazing"), which led to the emergence of the popular [maiolica](/wiki/Maiolica "Maiolica").[[135]](#cite_note-135)

One major emphasis in ceramic development in the Muslim world was the use of [tile](/wiki/Tile "Tile") and [decorative tilework](/wiki/Tile#Decorative_tilework_and_coloured_brick "Tile").

#### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Americas")]

Main article: [Ceramics of indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Ceramics_of_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Ceramics of indigenous peoples of the Americas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Sun_God_Effigy_Lid%2C_500-700_AD%2C_Maya_culture%2C_Mexico%2C_Guatemala%2C_or_Belize%2C_earthenware_-_Gardiner_Museum%2C_Toronto_-_DSC01179.JPG/250px-Sun_God_Effigy_Lid%2C_500-700_AD%2C_Maya_culture%2C_Mexico%2C_Guatemala%2C_or_Belize%2C_earthenware_-_Gardiner_Museum%2C_Toronto_-_DSC01179.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sun_God_Effigy_Lid,_500-700_AD,_Maya_culture,_Mexico,_Guatemala,_or_Belize,_earthenware_-_Gardiner_Museum,_Toronto_-_DSC01179.JPG)

Earthenware effigy of the Sun God. Maya culture, 500–700 CE

Most evidence points to an independent development of pottery in Native American cultures, with the earliest known dates in Brazil ranging from 9,500 to 5,000 years ago and from 7,000 to 6,000 years ago.[[7]](#cite_note-Roosevelt_1996_264–349-7) Further north in [Mesoamerica](/wiki/Mesoamerica "Mesoamerica"), dates begin with the [Archaic Era](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology#archaic "Mesoamerican chronology") (3500–2000 BC), and into the [Formative period](/wiki/Mesoamerican_chronology#Preclassic_Era_or_Formative_Period "Mesoamerican chronology") (2000 BC – AD 200). These cultures did not develop the stoneware, porcelain, or glazes found in the Old World. [Maya ceramics](/wiki/Maya_ceramics "Maya ceramics") include finely painted vessels, usually beakers, with elaborate scenes with several figures and texts. Several cultures, beginning with the [Olmec](/wiki/Olmec "Olmec"), made terracotta sculpture, and sculptural pieces depicting humans or animals that also serve as vessels are produced in many places, with [Moche portrait vessels](/wiki/Moche_portrait_vessel "Moche portrait vessel") among the finest.

#### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Reconstructed_lotiform_chalice%2C_public_domain_image_from_the_MET.jpg/250px-Reconstructed_lotiform_chalice%2C_public_domain_image_from_the_MET.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reconstructed_lotiform_chalice,_public_domain_image_from_the_MET.jpg)

Faience lotiform chalice. Egypt 1070–664 BCE ([reconstructed](/wiki/Lotiform_vessels_(Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art) "Lotiform vessels (Metropolitan Museum of Art)") from eight fragments)

The oldest pottery in the world outside of East Asia can be found in Africa. In 2007, Swiss archaeologists discovered pieces of some of the oldest pottery in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") at [Ounjougou](/wiki/Ounjougou "Ounjougou") in the central region of Mali, dating to at least 9,400 BC.[[6]](#cite_note-swissinfo-6) Excavations in the [Bosumpra Cave](/wiki/Bosumpra_Cave "Bosumpra Cave") on the [Kwahu Plateau](/wiki/Kwahu_Plateau "Kwahu Plateau") in southeastern Ghana, have revealed well-manufactured pottery using a variety of surface decoration techniques dating from the early tenth millennium cal. BC.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Following the emergence of [pottery traditions](#Africa) in the Ounjougou region of [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali") around 11,900 BP and in the Bosumpra region of [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") soon after, ceramics later arrived in the [Iho Eleru](/wiki/Iho_Eleru "Iho Eleru") region of [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria").[[137]](#cite_note-Cerasoni-137) In later periods, a relationship of the introduction of pot-making in some parts of [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") with the spread of [Bantu languages](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") has been long recognized, although the details remain controversial and awaiting further research, and no consensus has been reached.[[19]](#cite_note-jstor.org-19)

Use of pottery was then found in the [Bir Kiseiba region](/wiki/Bir_Kiseiba "Bir Kiseiba"), with a surplus of pottery shards dated roughly 9,300 BC. Archeological digs around [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") have continued to bring more history of ceramic use to light, including pottery shards found in Ravin de la Mouche, which were carbon-dated to roughly 7,500 BC. After 8,000 BC, the prevalence of ceramics in [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") surged, becoming a continent-wide phenomenon.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

#### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pottery&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Oceania")]

Pottery has been found at archaeological sites across the islands of Oceania and is attributed to an ancient culture known as the [Lapita](/wiki/Lapita "Lapita"). Another form of pottery, called *Plainware*, is found at sites throughout Oceania.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The relationship between Lapita pottery and Plainware is not altogether clear. The need for pottery eventually diminished with the settlement of islands farther east into Polynesia, as people there adapted to cooking with [earth ovens](/wiki/Earth_oven "Earth oven").[[139]](#cite_note-139)

The [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") never developed pottery.[[140]](#cite_note-140) After Europeans came to Australia and settled, they found deposits of clay which were analysed by English potters as excellent for making pottery. Less than 20 years later, Europeans arrived in Australia and began making pottery. Since then, ceramic manufacturing, mass-produced pottery, and studio pottery have flourished in Australia.[[141]](#cite_note-141)
